A data story
Not so Sweet
as we go deep — The Chocolate
Scroll to unwrap the truth behind a $5 bar
scroll
Five hands touched
this bar before you.
From the farmer who grew the beans to the retailer who sold it — each step took a cut. Scroll to follow the money.
Follow the money
The supply chain
Who gets how much / $5 ?
The last touched hand
Retailer
$1.40/
Shelf space, margin, no production cost. The supermarket takes the largest single cut without growing, making, or shipping a thing.
Walmart · Target · Amazon · Whole Foods
The 4th hand
Brand
$1.25/
They put their name on the wrapper, invest in marketing, and own the consumer relationship. Six companies control 54% of a $130 billion market.
Mars · Nestlé · Mondelēz · Hershey · Lindt
The 3rd hand
Processor
$0.90/
Beans become cocoa mass, butter, and powder here. Just three companies — Barry Callebaut, Cargill, Olam — control 60% of all global processing.
Barry Callebaut · Cargill · Olam
The 2nd hand
Trader & Exporter
$0.70/
Traders buy beans at origin prices and ship them to processors in Europe and North America. The value creation happens far from where the cocoa grows.
Sucden · Touton · Olam commodity arm
The first touched hand
Farmer
$0.23/
2.5 million smallholders in West Africa grow 65% of the world's cocoa — planting every tree, harvesting every pod. For a $5 bar, they take home only twenty-three cents.
Ivory Coast · Ghana · Cameroon · Ecuador
Where every $5 goes
The origin — where it all begins

65% of the world's cocoa
comes from two countries.

Ivory Coast and Ghana together supply nearly two thirds of global cocoa production — yet their farmers earn less than 6% of the value of a chocolate bar. This is where the chain starts.

"Behind every dot on this map is a smallholder farmer."
Ivory Coast · Ghana · West Africa

95 out of 100 cocoa farmers will never earn enough to live on.

This is not a gap in the data. It is a gap in conscience — built into every bar on every shelf.

A cocoa farmer sorts dried beans in Ivory Coast — the country that grows 45% of the world's cocoa. Source: Reuters
Plight of the First Hands
How much is $0.23 per bar?
A farmer needs cocoa from 1,000 bars worth of pods to earn one day's living wage.
$0.93
farmer / day
vs
$2.15
poverty line / day
The math is not complicated.
It is a choice.
The sixth hand nobody talks about
$0.23 wasn't enough.
So the children went to work.
When a family cannot survive on cocoa income, the choice is devastating — the children join the farm. Not out of tradition. Not by accident. Out of necessity manufactured by the supply chain above them.
1.56 million children work on West African cocoa farms today. They carry 50kg sacks, wield machetes, and are exposed to pesticides. 95% work in hazardous conditions. The average age is 12.
Every bar you have ever eaten was touched by a child who should have been in school.
Children breaking cocoa pods on a farm in West Africa. Source: ILO

Each point on this line is a survey count of children working on cocoa farms in West Africa. The amber markers are deadlines set by the chocolate industry's own Harkin-Engel Protocol — a 2001 pledge to eliminate child labour. Every single deadline passed. The line kept rising. By 2020, there were 14 percentage points more child labourers than when the promise was made.

The Betrayal
4 promises.
All broken. The farmer is still waiting.
In 2001, the world's largest chocolate companies — including Mars, Nestlé, and Hershey — signed the Harkin-Engel Protocol, a voluntary pledge to eliminate the worst forms of child labour from their West African cocoa supply chains. They set their own deadlines. They missed every one.
The industry that signed this protocol in 2001 generated $130 billion in revenue last year. The protocol cost them nothing. The children paid for it.
0 of 4
deadlines met
The reckoning
They had the money.
They made a choice.
The five brands below collectively earned $66 billion in combined revenue last year — half the $130B global market. Each one signed the Harkin-Engel Protocol. Each one missed every deadline. Below is what they chose to pay — and what a living wage would have cost them.
1 square. That's all it would cost.
Each square = $1.3B · 100 squares = $130B industry revenue · Red square = cost to close every farmer's wage gap
$130B
industry revenue / yr
$1.3B
cost to fix · every farmer · forever
23 yrs
they chose not to
How this gets solved
The problem is known.
So are the solutions.
None of these require a revolution. They require the industry to honour commitments it already made — and governments to stop waiting for it to self-regulate.
💰
Living Wage Certification
Fairtrade and Rainforest Alliance certifications guarantee a price floor above survival level. Currently only ~8% of cocoa is certified. Brands choosing certified sourcing directly raise farmer income.
Works now · 8% certified today
⚖️
Mandatory Due Diligence Laws
The EU Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (CSDDD) requires companies to identify and address human rights risks in their supply chains — including child labour. Voluntary pledges have failed. Law has teeth.
EU CSDDD · Enforcement begins 2027
🤝
Direct Trade Models
Some craft chocolate makers bypass traders entirely, paying farmers 3–5× the commodity price. This model is small-scale today but proves the supply chain can be restructured without the industry losing profitability.
Proven model · Scalable
📢
Consumer & Investor Pressure
Brands respond to reputational risk faster than regulation. Sustained public pressure — through purchasing choices, shareholder votes, and social media — has historically moved corporate supply chain policy more quickly than lobbying.
Consumer leverage · Proven track record
You are blinded by the wrapping,
but remember —
It's Not so Sweet as you go deep.
Every bar carries the same story.
What changes is what you do with it.
🛒
Buy certified
Look for Fairtrade, Rainforest Alliance, or Direct Trade labels. Certified bars pay farmers above the poverty line.
✉️
Write to a brand
Mars, Nestlé, Mondelēz, Hershey, Ferrero — ask them publicly when they will meet their 2001 promise.
🔗
Share this story
The industry relies on consumers not knowing. Every person who reads this is one fewer person they can count on staying silent.
Data sources: ICCO · ILO · Fairtrade International · EU CSDDD · Statista · Payson Center surveys · Harkin-Engel Protocol (2001)
